You are purchasing a new air conditioner. Each question refers to the market offerings for a particular product category in terms of a trade-off between price and one quality attribute.
contribution: Goal: Demonstrate experimentally that consumers rely partly on context to make inferences about the fit between specific brands and their own preferences. Findings: The inference effect accounts for two-thirds of the average magnitude of the context effect and for about one-half of the cross-category context-effect variance.

scenario: You are purchasing a six-pack of beer.
contribution: Goal: To propose and test that choice behavior under preference uncertainty can be explained by assuming consumers select the alternative supported by the best reasons, providing an explanation for the attraction effect and predicting a compromise effect. Findings: Brands tend to gain share when they become compromise alternatives; attraction and compromise effects tend to be stronger when subjects expect to justify decisions to others.

scenario: You are purchasing a computer.
contribution: Goal: To explore cognitive models of inferior, compromise, and phantom decoy effects in both judgment and choice. Findings: Both emergent-value and value-shift models of inferior decoy effects were supported, but only the emergent-value model of compromise decoy effects was supported. The phantom decoy effect was choice-based and did not occur in judgment.
